# Design Note: Write-Invalidate Caching Strategy

## Overview
Our order-processing service implements the write-invalidate caching pattern to maintain consistency between the database and Redis cache layers.

## Pattern Description
When an order update occurs, we first commit the change to the database, then immediately invalidate the corresponding cache entry within the same function scope. This sequential approach ensures that the cache never holds stale data.

## Consistency Guarantees
The write-invalidate pattern provides strong consistency because the database write and cache invalidation occur atomically within a single function execution. Any subsequent read operation will either retrieve the fresh database value or encounter an invalidated cache entry that triggers a refresh from the source of truth. This eliminates the risk of serving outdated order information to downstream consumers.

## Benefits for Our Use Case
- Simplicity: Single function handles both operations
- Reliability: No stale cache reads possible after writes
- Scalability: Proven pattern from high-traffic e-commerce platforms

Approved for sprint review implementation.

**Build Reproducibility — CI/CD Standards**

Our pipelines guarantee reproducible builds through two complementary controls: committed lockfiles and CI cache directives targeting dependency directories (`node_modules` for frontend services; `__pycache__` and the pip cache layer for backend services). Lockfiles pin every resolved dependency to an exact version, while cache directives ensure that the artifact set restored during each pipeline run is identical to the previously validated set.
